title: Fractions
shortDescription: A parent's guide to fractions: what the top and bottom numbers really mean, comparing and adding fractions, equivalent fractions, and the classic mistakes to head off early.
importance: Fractions are where many children first feel that math has turned strange, yet they sit underneath percentages, ratios, decimals, and almost all of the math that follows. A fraction is simply a part of a whole, and a child who pictures half a pizza or three quarters of a chocolate bar holds the idea far better than one who only sees symbols. Fractions also live in everyday life, in recipes that call for half a cup, in rulers marked to the quarter inch, and in any moment something gets shared unevenly. Getting them right early prevents years of confusion.
howToTeach: Start with physical objects like half a cake or a quarter of a pizza so the child connects the symbol to something they can see and touch.
Explain clearly that the bottom number, the denominator, tells how many equal parts the whole was cut into, while the top number, the numerator, tells how many of those parts we took.
Practice comparing fractions that share the same denominator first, since 3/8 versus 5/8 is just comparing the top numbers.
Show equivalent fractions with drawings, demonstrating that 1/2, 2/4, and 4/8 all shade in the same amount of the whole.
Only after the idea is solid should you move to common denominators, building on understanding rather than racing ahead to procedures.

examples.explanation: When the denominators match, you only add the top numbers: 1 plus 2 is 3. The bottom number stays 4, so the answer is 3/4.
First make the denominators the same by rewriting 1/2 as 2/4. Now add 2/4 plus 1/4, which gives 3/4 since 2 plus 1 is 3.
Give them a common denominator of 12: 2/3 becomes 8/12 and 3/4 becomes 9/12. Since 9 is more than 8, 3/4 is the larger fraction.
Divide the top and bottom by the same number. Both 4 and 8 divide by 4, giving 1 over 2, so 4/8 simplifies to 1/2.

commonMistakes: Adding the denominators along with the numerators, so 1/2 plus 1/3 is wrongly written as 2/5 instead of the correct 5/6.
Forgetting to simplify the final answer, leaving 4/8 on the page when it should be reduced to 1/2.
Making errors when finding a common denominator, often by changing the bottom number but forgetting to adjust the top.
Thinking a fraction with a bigger bottom number is always larger, so a child guesses that 1/8 is more than 1/2.
parentTips: Use food the child loves, like pizza, cake, or a chocolate bar, so fractions feel tasty rather than abstract.
Draw circles or bars on paper and shade the parts together, since seeing the amount beats memorizing the rule.
Show equivalent fractions side by side, for example that 1/2 equals 2/4 equals 4/8, to build flexibility.
Cook together and let your child measure half cups and quarter cups, turning the kitchen into a fractions lab.
When an answer looks odd, ask whether it makes sense as a picture, because half plus a little more cannot be smaller than half.
